name: Eric Dixon
abstract: Eric "Big Daddy" Dixon (March 28, 1930 – October 19, 1989) was an American jazz tenor saxophonist, flautist, composer, and arranger. Dixon's professional career extended from 1950 until his death in 1989, during which time he was credited on as many as 200 recordings.

concerts.label: Kool Jazz Festival: Ella Fitzgerald and Count Basie
Kool Jazz Festival: Ella Fitzgerald and Count Basie
JVC Jazz Festival: Nancy Wilson, Singer, and Joe Williams, Singer, with The Count Basie Orchestra

about_quotes.speaker: David N. Baker
about_quotes.evidence: I joined Quincy’s band... The saxophone section was Phil Woods, Gene Quill, Budd Johnson, Sahib Shihab, and – who is it? It might have been Joey Lopes. I can’t remember who the other saxophone player was. [It was] Eric Dixon
about_quotes.doc_title: David N. Baker — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Frank Foster
about_quotes.evidence: The arrangers who were in the band, namely Thad Jones, Frank Wess and myself, Ernie Wilkins while he was still there, and Eric, the late Eric Dixon, definitely had an advantage being in the band.
about_quotes.doc_title: Frank Foster interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, April 7, 1998

about_quotes.speaker: Frank Wess
about_quotes.evidence: I got Eddie Jones in the band, Bill Hughes, Sonny Cohn, Eric Dixon, Thad Jones... I told him to call Eric because you know I was playing third alto... So he called him and he stayed there.
about_quotes.doc_title: Frank Wess interviewed by Monk Rowe and Michael Woods, Majesty of the Seas, May 29, 1995

about_quotes.speaker: Wendell Brunious
about_quotes.evidence: I used to go up to his house every day. And he would go through some stuff... And one time we went to Detroit at the Pontchartrain Hotel.
about_quotes.doc_title: Wendell Brunious interviewed by Monk Rowe, Scottsdale, Arizona, April 16, 2000 [interview 2 of 2]

about_quotes.speaker: Wallace Davenport
about_quotes.evidence: Like one time when Eric Dixon, who was a tennis player, was going to play with Basie... I asked Eric, say, man, where is Wallace?

about_quotes.speaker: Frank Foster
about_quotes.evidence: the tenor saxophone chair that was ultimately occupied by Eddie “Lockjaw” Davis, Eric Dixon... In this case it was Eric Dixon.
about_quotes.doc_title: Frank Foster — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History
